[College Park, Maryland](/wiki/College_Park,_Maryland "College Park, Maryland") , United States  [38°59′17″N 76°56′33″W﻿ / ﻿38.9881°N 76.9425°W﻿ / 38.9881; -76.9425](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&params=38.9881_N_76.9425_W_region:US-MD_type:edu)
* [NCAA Division I FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FBS "NCAA Division I FBS") – [Big Ten](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference")

(**University of Maryland**, **UMD**, or simply **Maryland**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [land-grant](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [College Park, Maryland](/wiki/College_Park,_Maryland "College Park, Maryland"), United States near [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") Founded in 1856, UMD is the [flagship institution](/wiki/Flagship_university "Flagship university") of the [University System of Maryland](/wiki/University_System_of_Maryland "University System of Maryland") and is the largest university in the state of Maryland.

The **University of Maryland, College Park** (**University of Maryland**, **UMD**, or simply **Maryland**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [land-grant](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [College Park, Maryland](/wiki/College_Park,_Maryland "College Park, Maryland"), United States near [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") Founded in 1856, UMD is the [flagship institution](/wiki/Flagship_university "Flagship university") of the [University System of Maryland](/wiki/University_System_of_Maryland "University System of Maryland") and is the largest university in the state of Maryland.

UMD is the largest university in Maryland and in the [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"). Its eleven schools and colleges offer over 200 degree-granting programs, including 113 undergraduate majors, 107 [master's](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree") programs, and 83 [doctoral programs](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate"). UMD's athletic teams are known as the [Maryland Terrapins](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins "Maryland Terrapins") and compete in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") as a member of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference").

A member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities"), the University of Maryland's proximity to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") has resulted in many research partnerships with the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States");[[8]](#cite_note-8) faculty receive research funding and institutional support from many agencies, such as the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health"), [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA"), the [National Institute of Standards and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology"), the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration"), the [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency"), and the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – very high research activity"[[11]](#cite_note-Carnegie-11) and has been labeled a "[Public Ivy](/wiki/Public_Ivy "Public Ivy")".[[12]](#cite_note-12) According to the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), the university spent a combined $1.14 billion on research and development in 2021, ranking it 17th among American universities.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the University of Maryland, College Park](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_Maryland,_College_Park "History of the University of Maryland, College Park")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Charles_Benedict_Calvert_-_photo_portrait_seated.jpg/250px-Charles_Benedict_Calvert_-_photo_portrait_seated.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Benedict_Calvert_-_photo_portrait_seated.jpg)

[Charles Benedict Calvert](/wiki/Charles_Benedict_Calvert "Charles Benedict Calvert") (1808–1864), founder of the forerunner Maryland Agricultural College

On March 6, 1856, the forerunner of today's University of Maryland was chartered as the **Maryland Agricultural College**.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Two years later, [Charles Benedict Calvert](/wiki/Charles_Benedict_Calvert "Charles Benedict Calvert") (1808–1864), a future [U.S. Representative](/wiki/United_States_Representative "United States Representative") (Congressman) and descendant of the first [Lord Baltimore](/wiki/Baron_Baltimore "Baron Baltimore"), purchased 420 acres (1.7 km2) of the [Riversdale Mansion](/wiki/Riversdale_(Maryland) "Riversdale (Maryland)") estate nearby today's [College Park, Maryland](/wiki/College_Park,_Maryland "College Park, Maryland").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Later that year, Calvert founded the school and was the acting president from 1859 to 1860.[[17]](#cite_note-17) On October 5, 1859, the first 34 students entered the Maryland Agricultural College.[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1) The school became a [land grant college](/wiki/Land_grant_college "Land grant college") in February 1864.[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1)

### Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Civil War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/UMD_Morrill_Hall.JPG/250px-UMD_Morrill_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:UMD_Morrill_Hall.JPG)

[Morrill Hall](/wiki/Morrill_Hall_(University_of_Maryland) "Morrill Hall (University of Maryland)"), built in 1898, the oldest academic building on campus.

During the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), [Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") soldiers under Brigadier General [Bradley Tyler Johnson](/wiki/Bradley_Tyler_Johnson "Bradley Tyler Johnson") moved past the college on July 12, 1864, as part of Jubal Early's raid on Washington, D.C.[[18]](#cite_note-18) By the end of the war, financial problems forced the administrators to sell off 200 acres (81 ha) of land, and the continuing decline in enrollment sent the Maryland Agricultural College into bankruptcy. The campus was used as a boys' preparatory school for the next two years.[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1)

The Maryland legislature assumed half ownership of the school in 1866. The college thus became, in part, a state institution. By October 1867, the school reopened with 11 students. In 1868, the former Confederate admiral [Franklin Buchanan](/wiki/Franklin_Buchanan "Franklin Buchanan") was appointed president of the school. Enrollment grew to 80 at the time of his resignation, and the school soon paid off its debt. In 1873, [Samuel Jones](/wiki/Samuel_Jones_(Confederate_Army_officer) "Samuel Jones (Confederate Army officer)"), a former Confederate Major General, became president of the college.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Twenty years later, the federally-funded Agricultural Experiment Station was established there. During this same period, state laws granted the college regulatory powers in several areas—including controlling farm disease, inspecting feed, establishing a state weather bureau and geological survey, and housing the forestry board.[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1) [Morrill Hall](/wiki/Morrill_Hall_(University_of_Maryland) "Morrill Hall (University of Maryland)") (the oldest instructional building still in use on campus) was built the following year.[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1)

### Great Fire of 1912

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Great Fire of 1912")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/MAC_fire_1912.jpg/250px-MAC_fire_1912.jpg)](/wiki/File:MAC_fire_1912.jpg)

The Great Fire of 1912

On November 29, 1912, a fire destroyed student housing, school records, and most of the academic buildings, leaving only [Morrill Hall](/wiki/Morrill_Hall_(University_of_Maryland) "Morrill Hall (University of Maryland)") untouched. There were no injuries or fatalities, and all but two students returned to the university and insisted on classes continuing. The first building built after the fire was the Calvert Hall in 1914.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1) A new administration building was not built until the 1940s.[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1)

### Twentieth century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Twentieth century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Univ_of_Maryland_campus_1938.jpg/250px-Univ_of_Maryland_campus_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Univ_of_Maryland_campus_1938.jpg)

The University of Maryland campus in 1938

During [Phillips Lee Goldsborough](/wiki/Phillips_Lee_Goldsborough "Phillips Lee Goldsborough")'s tenure as [Governor of Maryland](/wiki/Governor_of_Maryland "Governor of Maryland"), the state purchased Maryland Agricultural College, taking control of the school in 1916 and renaming it **Maryland State College**.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the same year, the first female students, Elizabeth Gambrill Hook and Charlotte Ann Vaux, enrolled at the school.[[22]](#cite_note-22) On April 9, 1920, the college became part of the existing [University of Maryland](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_Baltimore "University of Maryland, Baltimore"), replacing [St. John's College, Annapolis](/wiki/St._John%27s_College_(Annapolis/Santa_Fe) "St. John's College (Annapolis/Santa Fe)") as the university's undergraduate campus.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) In the same year, the graduate school on the College Park campus awarded its first Ph.D. degrees and the university's enrollment reached 500 students. In 1925 the university was accredited by the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1)

By the time the first black students enrolled at the university in 1951, enrollment had grown to nearly 10,000 students—4,000 of whom were women. Before 1951, many black students in Maryland were enrolled at the [University of Maryland, Eastern Shore](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_Eastern_Shore "University of Maryland, Eastern Shore").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 1957, President [Wilson H. Elkins](/wiki/Wilson_Homer_Elkins "Wilson Homer Elkins") pushed to increase the university's academic standards. His efforts resulted in creating one of the first Academic Probation Plans. The first year the plan went into effect, 1,550 students (18% of the total student body) faced expulsion.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

On October 19, 1957, [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") of the United Kingdom attended her first and only college football game at the University of Maryland after expressing interest in seeing a typically American sport during her first tour of the United States. The [Maryland Terrapins](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins "Maryland Terrapins") beat the [North Carolina Tar Heels](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels "North Carolina Tar Heels") 21 to 7 in the historical game now referred to as "The Queen's Game".[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

[Phi Beta Kappa](/wiki/Phi_Beta_Kappa "Phi Beta Kappa") established a chapter at The University of Maryland in 1964. In 1969, the university was elected to the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities"). The school continued to grow, and by the fall of 1985 reached an enrollment of 38,679.[[1]](#cite_note-umtimeline-1) Like many colleges during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), the university was the site of student protests and had curfews enforced by the [National Guard](/wiki/Maryland_Army_National_Guard "Maryland Army National Guard").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In a massive restructuring of the state's higher education system in 1988, the school was designated as the flagship campus of the newly formed [University of Maryland System](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_System "University of Maryland System") (later changed to the University System of Maryland in 1997). It was formally named the University of Maryland, College Park. All five campuses in the former network were designated distinct campuses in the new system. However, in 1997 the [Maryland General Assembly](/wiki/Maryland_General_Assembly "Maryland General Assembly") passed legislation allowing the University of Maryland, College Park, to be known simply as the University of Maryland, recognizing the campus' role as the flagship institution of the University System of Maryland.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 1994, the [National Archives at College Park](/wiki/National_Archives_at_College_Park "National Archives at College Park") completed construction and opened on a parcel of land adjoining the campus donated by the University of Maryland, after lobbying by President [William Kirwan](/wiki/William_Kirwan "William Kirwan") and congressional leaders to foster academic collaboration between the institutions.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Twenty-first century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Twenty-first century")]

In 2004, the university began constructing the 150-acre (61 ha) "M Square Research Park", which includes facilities affiliated with the [U.S. Department of Defense](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Defense "U.S. Department of Defense"), [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration"), and the new National Center for Weather and Climate Prediction, affiliated with the [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") (NOAA).[[33]](#cite_note-33) In May 2010, ground was broken on a new Physical Science Complex, including an advanced quantum science laboratory.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The university suffered multiple [data breaches](/wiki/Data_breach "Data breach") in 2014. The first resulted in the compromise of over 300,000 student and faculty records.[[35]](#cite_note-35) A second data breach occurred several months later.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The second breach was investigated by the FBI and Secret Service and found to be done by David Helkowski.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Despite the attribution, no charges were filed. As a result of the data breaches, the university offered free credit protection for five years to the students and faculty affected.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In 2017, the university received a record-breaking donation of $219.5 million from the [A. James & Alice B. Clark Foundation](/wiki/A._James_Clark "A. James Clark"), ranking among the country's largest philanthropic gifts to a public university.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) [Darryll J. Pines](/wiki/Darryll_J._Pines "Darryll J. Pines") became the 34th president of the university in 2020. Pines was a professor of Aerospace Engineering at the university before becoming president.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 2021, the university announced it had raised $1.5 billion in donations since 2018.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In April 2024, UMD students joined other [campuses across the United States in protests](/wiki/List_of_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses_in_the_United_States_in_2024 "List of pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses in the United States in 2024") against the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"). Students called attention to the [genocide in Palestine](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide") and for the university to [divest from companies](/wiki/Boycott,_Divestment_and_Sanctions "Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions") that support the Gaza war.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Activism continued in the next academic year with the placement of small flags in the lawn on McKeldin Mall, representing the more than 150,000 Palestinians killed in Gaza.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Path_on_the_left_hand_side_of_McKeldin_mall_in_winter.JPG/250px-Path_on_the_left_hand_side_of_McKeldin_mall_in_winter.JPG)](/wiki/File:Path_on_the_left_hand_side_of_McKeldin_mall_in_winter.JPG)

The center of the university's 1,250 acres (5.1 km2) is McKeldin Mall, which is the largest academic mall in the United States.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Mall is bordered on the west and east by [McKeldin Library](/wiki/McKeldin_Library "McKeldin Library") and the Thomas V. Miller, Jr. Administration Building, respectively. Academic buildings surround McKeldin Mall on the north and south ends. They are the homes to many departments in the [College of Behavioral and Social Sciences](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_College_of_Behavioral_and_Social_Sciences "University of Maryland College of Behavioral and Social Sciences"), College of Arts and Humanities, and the College of Agriculture and Natural Resources. South of McKeldin Mall are the North Hill Community,[[52]](#cite_note-52) at the southwesterly corner, and the original center of campus, [Morrill Hall](/wiki/Morrill_Hall_(University_of_Maryland) "Morrill Hall (University of Maryland)") and the Morrill Quad. South of the Morrill Quad are the South Hill and South Campus Commons Communities, and the Southwest Mall and the [Robert H. Smith School of Business](/wiki/Robert_H._Smith_School_of_Business "Robert H. Smith School of Business") to the southwest.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Running parallel to McKeldin Mall to the north is Campus Drive, the main thoroughfare through campus. Another thoroughfare, Regents Drive, runs perpendicular to the Mall and is home to the [Memorial Chapel](/wiki/Memorial_Chapel_(University_of_Maryland) "Memorial Chapel (University of Maryland)") and the Campus Farms. Regents Drive crosses Campus Drive at the campus hallmark, [The "M"](/wiki/The_%22M%22 "The \"M\""), which is a mound with a large "M" formed by flowers in its center.[[54]](#cite_note-54) The northeast quadrant of campus, formed by Campus and Regent Drives, is home to many of [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences") and [applied sciences](/wiki/Applied_sciences "Applied sciences") departments. [The Rossborough Inn](/wiki/The_Rossborough_Inn "The Rossborough Inn"), which, was built during the years 1798 to 1812, is the oldest building on campus (and is older than the university itself).[[55]](#cite_note-55) There are five regularly used entrances to campus; the main entrance, off of Baltimore Avenue and onto Campus Drive, is referred to as North Gate and features the Gatehouse, an ornate gateway honoring the university's founders.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The 140-acre (57 ha), 18-hole [University of Maryland Golf Course](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_Golf_Course "University of Maryland Golf Course") sits at the northern edge of campus, as does [the Observatory](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_Observatory "University of Maryland Observatory").[[57]](#cite_note-57)

The campus contains 7,500 documented trees and garden plantings, leading the [American Public Gardens Association](/wiki/American_Public_Gardens_Association "American Public Gardens Association") to designate the campus the [University of Maryland Arboretum & Botanical Garden](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_Arboretum_%26_Botanical_Garden "University of Maryland Arboretum & Botanical Garden") in 2008.[[58]](#cite_note-universityvisitorsnetwork1-58) There are also nearly 400 acres (1.6 km2) of [urban forest](/wiki/Urban_forest "Urban forest") on campus[[58]](#cite_note-universityvisitorsnetwork1-58) and the [Arbor Day Foundation](/wiki/Arbor_Day_Foundation "Arbor Day Foundation") has named the university to its 'Tree Campus USA' list.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The recreational Paint Branch Trail, part of the [Anacostia Tributary Trails](/wiki/Anacostia_Tributary_Trails "Anacostia Tributary Trails") system, cuts through campus, as does the [Paint Branch](/wiki/Paint_Branch "Paint Branch") stream, a tributary of the [Northeast Branch Anacostia River](/wiki/Northeast_Branch_Anacostia_River "Northeast Branch Anacostia River").[[60]](#cite_note-60) The university's first [Leed Gold](/wiki/Leed_Gold "Leed Gold") building, Knight Hall, opened in April 2010 as the new home for the [Philip Merrill College of Journalism](/wiki/Philip_Merrill_College_of_Journalism "Philip Merrill College of Journalism").[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) In 2021, President Pines pledged that the University of Maryland would achieve [carbon neutrality](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") by [Earth Day](/wiki/Earth_Day "Earth Day") 2025.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Academics

Admission to Maryland is rated "most selective" by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) For the Class of 2026 (enrolled fall 2022), Maryland received 56,766 applications and accepted 19,451 (34.3%). Of those accepted, 4,742 enrolled, a [yield rate](/wiki/Yield_(college_admissions) "Yield (college admissions)") (the percentage of accepted students who choose to attend the university) of 24.4%.[[66]](#cite_note-FallEnrollmentReport-66) Maryland's freshman [retention rate](/wiki/University_student_retention "University student retention") is 95.5%, with 88.3% going on to graduate within six years.[[66]](#cite_note-FallEnrollmentReport-66)

Maryland is a test-optional university and students can decide whether or not to submit SAT or ACT scores.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Of the 34% of the incoming freshman class who submitted [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") scores; the middle 50 percent Composite scores were 1340–1490.[[66]](#cite_note-FallEnrollmentReport-66) Of the 9% of enrolled freshmen in 2021 who submitted [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(test) "ACT (test)") scores; the middle 50 percent Composite score was between 30 and 34.[[66]](#cite_note-FallEnrollmentReport-66)

The University of Maryland, College Park is a college sponsor of the National Merit Scholarship Program and sponsored 58 Merit Scholarship awards in 2020. In the 2020–2021 academic year, 69 freshman students were [National Merit Scholars](/wiki/National_Merit_Scholars "National Merit Scholars").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Faculty")]

Main article: [List of University of Maryland, College Park people § Faculty](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Maryland,_College_Park_people#Faculty "List of University of Maryland, College Park people")

The university's faculty has included four Nobel Prize laureates. The earliest recipient, in 1956, was [Juan Ramón Jiménez](/wiki/Juan_Ram%C3%B3n_Jim%C3%A9nez "Juan Ramón Jiménez"), a Spanish language and literature professor. Four decades later, physics professor [William Daniel Phillips](/wiki/William_Daniel_Phillips "William Daniel Phillips") won a prize in physics for his contributions to [laser cooling](/wiki/Laser_cooling "Laser cooling"). In 2005, [Thomas Schelling](/wiki/Thomas_Schelling "Thomas Schelling") was awarded the prize in economics for his contributions to [game theory](/wiki/Game_theory "Game theory"). In 2006, [John C. Mather](/wiki/John_C._Mather "John C. Mather") was awarded the prize in physics alongside [George Smoot](/wiki/George_Smoot "George Smoot") for their work in the discovery of blackbody form and [anisotropy](/wiki/Anisotropy "Anisotropy") of the [cosmic microwave background radiation](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background_radiation "Cosmic microwave background radiation"). In addition, two University of Maryland alumni are Nobel Prize laureates; [Herbert Hauptman](/wiki/Herbert_Hauptman "Herbert Hauptman") won the 1985 prize in chemistry, and [Raymond Davis Jr.](/wiki/Raymond_Davis_Jr. "Raymond Davis Jr.") won the 2002 prize in physics.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)

The university has many notable academics. Professor of mathematics, [Sergei Novikov](/wiki/Sergei_Novikov_(mathematician) "Sergei Novikov (mathematician)") won the [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal") in 1970, followed by alumnus [Charles Fefferman](/wiki/Charles_Fefferman "Charles Fefferman") in 1978. Alumnus [George Dantzig](/wiki/George_Dantzig "George Dantzig") won the 1975 [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") for his work in the field of linear programming. Professor of physics [Michael Fisher](/wiki/Michael_Fisher "Michael Fisher") won the [Wolf Prize](/wiki/Wolf_Prize "Wolf Prize") in 1980 (together with [Kenneth G. Wilson](/wiki/Kenneth_G._Wilson "Kenneth G. Wilson") and [Leo Kadanoff](/wiki/Leo_Kadanoff "Leo Kadanoff")) and the [IUPAP](/wiki/IUPAP "IUPAP") [Boltzmann Medal](/wiki/Boltzmann_Medal "Boltzmann Medal") in 1983. [James A. Yorke](/wiki/James_A._Yorke "James A. Yorke"), a distinguished university professor of mathematics and physics and chair of the mathematics department, won the 2003 [Japan Prize](/wiki/Japan_Prize "Japan Prize") for his work in [chaotic systems](/wiki/Chaotic_systems "Chaotic systems"). In 2013, professor of physics [Sylvester James Gates](/wiki/Sylvester_James_Gates "Sylvester James Gates") was awarded the [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science").[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Research")]

UMD is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity".[[11]](#cite_note-Carnegie-11) In FY 2020, the university spent about 1.103 billion dollars in total R&D expenditures, ranking it 16th in the nation.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

On October 14, 2004, the university added 150 acres (61 ha) in an attempt to create the largest research park inside the Washington, D.C. [Capital Beltway](/wiki/Interstate_495_(Capital_Beltway) "Interstate 495 (Capital Beltway)"), formerly known as "M Square" and now known as the "Discovery District."[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Glenn_L._Martin_Institute_of_Technology_at_UMCP%2C_2007.jpg/250px-Glenn_L._Martin_Institute_of_Technology_at_UMCP%2C_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glenn_L._Martin_Institute_of_Technology_at_UMCP,_2007.jpg)

Glenn L. Martin Institute of Technology

Many of the faculty members have funding from federal agencies such as the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health"),[[98]](#cite_note-98) [NASA](/wiki/National_Aeronautics_and_Space_Administration "National Aeronautics and Space Administration"),[[99]](#cite_note-99) the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security"),[[100]](#cite_note-100) the [National Institute of Standards and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology"), and the [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency").

The [Space Systems Laboratory](/wiki/Space_Systems_Laboratory_(Maryland) "Space Systems Laboratory (Maryland)") researches human-robotic interaction for [astronautics](/wiki/Astronautics "Astronautics") applications and includes the only [neutral buoyancy](/wiki/Neutral_buoyancy "Neutral buoyancy") facility at a university.[[101]](#cite_note-101) The [Joint Global Change Research Institute](/wiki/Joint_Global_Change_Research_Institute "Joint Global Change Research Institute"), which studies human and earth systems, was formed in 2001 by the University of Maryland and the [Pacific Northwest National Laboratory](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_National_Laboratory "Pacific Northwest National Laboratory").[[102]](#cite_note-102)

The [National Consortium for the Study of Terrorism and Responses to Terrorism](/wiki/National_Consortium_for_the_Study_of_Terrorism_and_Responses_to_Terrorism "National Consortium for the Study of Terrorism and Responses to Terrorism") (START) launched in 2005 as one of the [Centers of Excellence](/wiki/Homeland_Security_Centers_of_Excellence "Homeland Security Centers of Excellence") supported by the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/Department_of_Homeland_Security "Department of Homeland Security") in the United States. START is focused on the scientific study of the causes and consequences of terrorism in the United States and worldwide.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### Living-learning programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Living-learning programs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/UMD_stairway.JPG/250px-UMD_stairway.JPG)](/wiki/File:UMD_stairway.JPG)

A stairway in South Campus

The university hosts "living-learning" programs (LLPs) that allow students with similar academic interests to live in the same residential community take specialized courses and perform research in those areas of expertise. These include CIVICUS, focused on politics and community service;[[104]](#cite_note-104) [Hinman](/wiki/Brian_Hinman "Brian Hinman") CEOs, an entrepreneurship program;[[105]](#cite_note-105) and the Language House, where students learning a shared target language live together.[[106]](#cite_note-106) Several LLPs exist under the university's Honors College, with focuses in topics including cybersecurity, entrepreneurship, and life sciences.[[107]](#cite_note-107) College Park Scholars is another LLP umbrella that includes programs in the arts, public health, and legal thought, among others.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Residential life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Residential life")]

There are two main residential areas on campus, North Campus and South Campus. North Campus is made up of Cambridge Community (which consists of five residence halls and houses the College Park Scholars program), Denton Community (which consists of three halls), Oakland Community (which consists of one hall), Ellicott Community (consisting of three halls), and the Courtyards, a garden-style apartment community in north campus consisting of seven buildings. The Heritage community, completed in 2024, features two residence halls and a dining hall (Johnson Whittle Hall, Pyon Chen Hall, and Yahentamitsi Dining Hall.)[[110]](#cite_note-112)[[111]](#cite_note-113) Most residence halls have AC, but some do not. These non-AC halls are Caroline, Carroll, Cecil, Chestertown, Ellicott, Hagerstown, Wicomico, and Worcester.[[112]](#cite_note-114)

South Campus includes the North Hill Community, made up of nine [Georgian-style](/wiki/Georgian-style "Georgian-style") halls and Prince Frederick Hall (which opened in 2014) immediately west of [McKeldin Mall](/wiki/McKeldin_Mall "McKeldin Mall");[[113]](#cite_note-115) South Hill Community, made up of fourteen small residence halls for upper-level students;[[114]](#cite_note-116) Leonardtown Community, which offers apartment-style housing; and the South Campus Commons Community, which consists of seven apartment-style buildings (the seventh and most recent building being opened in January 2010).[[115]](#cite_note-117)

### Dining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Dining")]

There are three dining halls on campus. In addition, [a food court in the Stamp Student Union](/wiki/Adele_H._Stamp_Student_Union#Dining "Adele H. Stamp Student Union") provides many fast food dining options for the university community.[[116]](#cite_note-118)[[117]](#cite_note-119) The 251 North dining hall lies in the Denton Community on the northern part of campus. The second northern dining hall, Yahentamitsi, is the first building on campus named in honor of Indigenous people which is the newest dining hall and is located next to the freshman dorms such as Johnson Whittle and Pyon-Chen.[[118]](#cite_note-120) The third dining hall, South Campus Dining Hall, can be found just south of McKeldin Library.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/College_Park_Station_91121.jpg/250px-College_Park_Station_91121.jpg)](/wiki/File:College_Park_Station_91121.jpg)

[College Park-University of Maryland](/wiki/College_Park-University_of_Maryland_(Washington_Metro) "College Park-University of Maryland (Washington Metro)") Metro station provides access to [Downtown, Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Downtown,_Washington,_D.C. "Downtown, Washington, D.C.")

The university is accessible through [the three airports](/wiki/Transportation_in_Washington,_D.C.#Air "Transportation in Washington, D.C.") in the greater [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"): [Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_Washington_National_Airport "Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport"), [Washington Dulles International Airport](/wiki/Washington_Dulles_International_Airport "Washington Dulles International Airport"), and [Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport](/wiki/Baltimore/Washington_International_Airport "Baltimore/Washington International Airport").[[120]](#cite_note-122) A small public airport in College Park, [College Park Airport](/wiki/College_Park_Airport "College Park Airport"), lies nearly adjacent to campus, but operations are limited. This airport is the world's oldest continually operating airport[[121]](#cite_note-afm-123) and the site of many significant aviation firsts.[[122]](#cite_note-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125)

A free shuttle service, known as [Shuttle–UM](/wiki/Shuttle%E2%80%93UM "Shuttle–UM"), is available for UMD students, faculty, staff, and some residents of College Park and Greenbelt.[[124]](#cite_note-126)[[125]](#cite_note-127) The university is served by an off-campus stop on the [Washington Metro](/wiki/Washington_Metro "Washington Metro")'s [Green](/wiki/Green_Line_(Washington_Metro) "Green Line (Washington Metro)") Line[[126]](#cite_note-128) called [College Park – University of Maryland](/wiki/College_Park_%E2%80%93_University_of_Maryland_(Washington_Metro) "College Park – University of Maryland (Washington Metro)"). This stop is adjacent to a stop on the [MARC](/wiki/MARC_Train "MARC Train")[[127]](#cite_note-129) Camden line, a commuter rail line which runs from Baltimore to Washington, D.C. The stop is also serviced by busses from Shuttle–UM, [MTA](/wiki/Maryland_Transit_Administration "Maryland Transit Administration"), [WMATA](/wiki/Washington_Metropolitan_Area_Transit_Authority "Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority"), and [TheBus](/wiki/TheBus_(Prince_George%27s_County) "TheBus (Prince George's County)").

The campus is also planned to be served by the [Purple Line](/wiki/Purple_Line_(Maryland) "Purple Line (Maryland)"), a light rail line under construction by the [Maryland Transit Administration](/wiki/Maryland_Transit_Administration "Maryland Transit Administration") connecting College Park with other inner suburbs of Maryland as well as the [Red](/wiki/Red_Line_(Washington_Metro) "Red Line (Washington Metro)") and [Orange](/wiki/Orange_Line_(Washington_Metro) "Orange Line (Washington Metro)") Lines of the Washington Metro.[[128]](#cite_note-dbknews.com-130) The Purple Line route will have five stops on and around the university's campus: M Square, the College Park Metro station, the main entrance to the campus on Route 1, near [Stamp Student Union](/wiki/Adele_H._Stamp_Student_Union "Adele H. Stamp Student Union") on Campus Drive, and on the other edge of campus on Adelphi Road, along with a parallel bike path.[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133)

### *The Diamondback*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: The Diamondback")]

Main article: [The Diamondback](/wiki/The_Diamondback "The Diamondback")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/UMD_Stamp_co-op%2C_food_court_beams.jpg/250px-UMD_Stamp_co-op%2C_food_court_beams.jpg)](/wiki/File:UMD_Stamp_co-op,_food_court_beams.jpg)

Atrium of [Stamp Student Union](/wiki/Stamp_Student_Union "Stamp Student Union"), near the food court and co-op

*The Diamondback* is an independent [student newspaper](/wiki/Student_newspaper "Student newspaper"). It was founded in 1910 as *The Triangle* and renamed in 1921 in honor of a local reptile, the [Diamondback terrapin](/wiki/Diamondback_terrapin "Diamondback terrapin"), which became the school mascot in 1933. The newspaper is published online daily during the spring and fall semesters, with a circulation of 17,000 and annual advertising revenues of over $1 million.[[132]](#cite_note-134) Notable journalists who have been with the paper include [David Simon](/wiki/David_Simon_(writer) "David Simon (writer)") of [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO")'s *[The Wire](/wiki/The_Wire "The Wire")* and [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s *[Homicide: Life on the Street](/wiki/Homicide:_Life_on_the_Street "Homicide: Life on the Street")*, and cartoonists [Jeff Kinney](/wiki/Jeff_Kinney_(writer) "Jeff Kinney (writer)"), who created the *[Diary of a Wimpy Kid](/wiki/Diary_of_a_Wimpy_Kid "Diary of a Wimpy Kid")* fiction series and whose *Igdoof* strip appeared in *The Diamondback*; [Aaron McGruder](/wiki/Aaron_McGruder "Aaron McGruder"), who first published his cartoon *[The Boondocks](/wiki/The_Boondocks_(TV_series) "The Boondocks (TV series)")* in *The Diamondback*; and [Frank Cho](/wiki/Frank_Cho "Frank Cho"), who began his career with the popular *University Squared* for *The Diamondback*.[[133]](#cite_note-WashCityPaper-135)

### Other student activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Other student activities")]

[WMUC-FM](/wiki/WMUC-FM "WMUC-FM") (90.5 FM) is the university's non-commercial radio station, staffed by UMD students and volunteers. WMUC is a [freeform](/wiki/Freeform_radio "Freeform radio") and [sports broadcasting](/wiki/Sports_broadcasting "Sports broadcasting") station broadcast at 10 watts. Its broadcasts can be heard throughout the [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"). Notable WMUC alumni include [Connie Chung](/wiki/Connie_Chung "Connie Chung"), [Bonnie Bernstein](/wiki/Bonnie_Bernstein "Bonnie Bernstein"), [Peter Rosenberg](/wiki/Peter_Rosenberg "Peter Rosenberg") and Aaron McGruder.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/UMCP_Administration_building%2C_seen_from_end_of_reflecting_pool_at_morning%2C_August_21%2C_2006.jpg/250px-UMCP_Administration_building%2C_seen_from_end_of_reflecting_pool_at_morning%2C_August_21%2C_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:UMCP_Administration_building,_seen_from_end_of_reflecting_pool_at_morning,_August_21,_2006.jpg)

Approximately 16% of men and women in Maryland's undergraduate student body were involved in [fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities") in 2017.[[134]](#cite_note-136)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Maryland Terrapins](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins "Maryland Terrapins")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Comcast_Center_interior.jpg/250px-Comcast_Center_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comcast_Center_interior.jpg)

[XFINITY Center](/wiki/XFINITY_Center_(College_Park,_Maryland) "XFINITY Center (College Park, Maryland)"), home of Maryland basketball

The university sponsors varsity athletic teams in 20 men's and women's sports. The teams, named the "Terrapins", represent Maryland in [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") competition. Maryland became a founding member of the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") in 1952 but left to join the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") on July 1, 2014. As of 2025, Maryland's athletic teams have been awarded 47 national championships by the NCAA, [USILA](/wiki/United_States_Intercollegiate_Lacrosse_Association "United States Intercollegiate Lacrosse Association"), [AIAW](/wiki/Association_for_Intercollegiate_Athletics_for_Women "Association for Intercollegiate Athletics for Women"), and [NCA](/wiki/National_Cheerleaders_Association "National Cheerleaders Association").[[135]](#cite_note-137)[[136]](#cite_note-138) In 2008 and 2010, *[The Princeton Review](/wiki/The_Princeton_Review "The Princeton Review")* named the University of Maryland's athletic facilities the best in the nation.[[137]](#cite_note-139)[[138]](#cite_note-140) The Terrapins nickname (often shortened to "Terps") was coined by former university president, football coach, and athletic director [H. C. "Curly" Byrd](/wiki/Curly_Byrd "Curly Byrd") in 1932.[[139]](#cite_note-141) The mascot is a [diamondback terrapin](/wiki/Diamondback_terrapin "Diamondback terrapin") named *[Testudo](/wiki/Testudo_(mascot) "Testudo (mascot)")*, which is [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") for "tortoise".[[140]](#cite_note-142) Since the early 20th century, the school athletic colors have been some combination of those on the [Maryland state flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Maryland "Flag of Maryland"): red, white, black, and gold.[[141]](#cite_note-143) Maryland is the only NCAA Division I school to have four official school colors.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

### Basketball and football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Basketball and football")]

[Men's basketball](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins_men%27s_basketball "Maryland Terrapins men's basketball") is the most popular sport at the university.[[143]](#cite_note-crawler-145) Long-time head coach [Lefty Driesell](/wiki/Lefty_Driesell "Lefty Driesell") began the now nationwide tradition of "[Midnight Madness](/wiki/Midnight_Madness_(basketball) "Midnight Madness (basketball)")" in 1971.[[144]](#cite_note-146) Beginning in 1989, alumnus [Gary Williams](/wiki/Gary_Williams "Gary Williams") revived the program, which was struggling in the wake of [Len Bias](/wiki/Len_Bias "Len Bias")'s death and NCAA rules infractions. Williams led Maryland basketball to national prominence with two [Final Four](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") appearances, and in 2002, a [national championship](/wiki/2002_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2002 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"). On February 7, 2006, Williams won his 349th game to surpass Driesell and became Maryland's all-time leader among basketball coaches.

[Maryland football](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins_football "Maryland Terrapins football") is also popular at the university.[[143]](#cite_note-crawler-145)

### Lacrosse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Lacrosse")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/D1_Lacrosse.jpg/250px-D1_Lacrosse.jpg)](/wiki/File:D1_Lacrosse.jpg)

[Maryland](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins_men%27s_lacrosse "Maryland Terrapins men's lacrosse") fields one of the nation's premier [lacrosse](/wiki/Field_lacrosse "Field lacrosse") programs.

[Maryland men's lacrosse](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins_men%27s_lacrosse "Maryland Terrapins men's lacrosse") remains one of the sport's top programs since its beginnings as a squad in 1865.[[145]](#cite_note-147) The team most recently won the national championship in [2022](/wiki/2022_NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Lacrosse_Championship "2022 NCAA Division I Men's Lacrosse Championship"), completing an undefeated season, the first since Virginia in 2006, and the first to go undefeated across 18 games. The team has won ten [USILA](/wiki/United_States_Intercollegiate_Lacrosse_Association "United States Intercollegiate Lacrosse Association") and NCAA national championships since its promotion to varsity status in 1924 and is a regular fixture in the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Lacrosse_Championship "NCAA Division I Men's Lacrosse Championship").[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[147]](#cite_note-149) The [Maryland women's lacrosse](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins_women%27s_lacrosse "Maryland Terrapins women's lacrosse") team has won 15 national championships, the most of any program in the nation.[[148]](#cite_note-150) The team has produced the National Player of the Year/[Tewaaraton Award](/wiki/Tewaaraton_Award "Tewaaraton Award") winner eight times, more than any other collegiate program.[[149]](#cite_note-151) The Terrapins have also made the most NCAA tournament appearances, won the most tournament games, and made the most NCAA championship game appearances of any program.[[150]](#cite_note-152) They most recently won the NCAA championship in 2019.

### Soccer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Soccer")]

The [men's soccer team](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins_men%27s_soccer "Maryland Terrapins men's soccer") has won four [NCAA Division I College Cup](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Soccer_Championship "NCAA Division I Men's Soccer Championship") national championships, most recently in 2018.[[151]](#cite_note-153) Under the guidance of head coach [Sasho Cirovski](/wiki/Sasho_Cirovski "Sasho Cirovski"), the soccer team has reached nine Final Fours and won three College Cups since 1997. The soccer team has developed a large, devoted fan base among students and the local community. The attendance record at Ludwig Field was set in 2015 when 8,449 fans saw Maryland win over top-ranked UCLA in extra time.[[152]](#cite_note-154) The annual total attendance increased dramatically from 12,710 in 1995 to 35,631 in 2008.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

### Field hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Field hockey")]

The [Maryland field hockey](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins_field_hockey "Maryland Terrapins field hockey") team has won a total of eight [NCAA national championships](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Field_Hockey_Championship "NCAA Division I Field Hockey Championship") and 13 conference championships (10 in the ACC and 5 in the Big Ten).[[154]](#cite_note-156)

### Marching band

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Maryland,_College_Park&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Marching band")]

The [Mighty Sound of Maryland](/wiki/Mighty_Sound_of_Maryland "Mighty Sound of Maryland") marching band attends all home football games and provides pre-game performances.[[155]](#cite_note-157) During basketball season, the marching band provides music in the stands.[[156]](#cite_note-158)

Notable alumni include [House Democratic Whip](/wiki/Party_leaders_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Party leaders of the United States House of Representatives") [Steny Hoyer](/wiki/Steny_Hoyer "Steny Hoyer");[[157]](#cite_note-159) Google co-founder [Sergey Brin](/wiki/Sergey_Brin "Sergey Brin");[[158]](#cite_note-160) [The Muppets](/wiki/The_Muppets "The Muppets") creator [Jim Henson](/wiki/Jim_Henson "Jim Henson");[[159]](#cite_note-161) *[The Wire](/wiki/The_Wire "The Wire")* creator [David Simon](/wiki/David_Simon "David Simon");[[160]](#cite_note-162) former NFL Quarterback [Norman "Boomer" Esiason](/wiki/Boomer_Esiason "Boomer Esiason"); [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") host [Gayle King](/wiki/Gayle_King "Gayle King"); journalist [Connie Chung](/wiki/Connie_Chung "Connie Chung"); and *[Seinfeld](/wiki/Seinfeld "Seinfeld")* co-creator and *[Curb Your Enthusiasm](/wiki/Curb_Your_Enthusiasm "Curb Your Enthusiasm")* creator [Larry David](/wiki/Larry_David "Larry David").[[161]](#cite_note-163) Prominent alumni in business include [Ed Snider](/wiki/Ed_Snider "Ed Snider"), former chairman of [Comcast Spectacor](/wiki/Comcast_Spectacor "Comcast Spectacor") and former owner of the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") and [Reena Aggarwal](/wiki/Reena_Aggarwal_(academic) "Reena Aggarwal (academic)"), Robert E. McDonough Professor of Finance at [Georgetown University](/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University") and Director of the Psaros Center for Financial Markets and Policy; journalist [Jim Walton](/wiki/Jim_Walton_(journalist) "Jim Walton (journalist)"), former president and CEO of [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"); [Kevin Plank](/wiki/Kevin_Plank "Kevin Plank"), founder and executive chairman of the athletic apparel company [Under Armour](/wiki/Under_Armour "Under Armour"); [Chris Kubasik](/wiki/Chris_Kubasik "Chris Kubasik"), former president of [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin"); and [Carly Fiorina](/wiki/Carly_Fiorina "Carly Fiorina"), former CEO of [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard"). Journalist [Carl Bernstein](/wiki/Carl_Bernstein "Carl Bernstein"), who won the [Pulitzer Prize for Public Service](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Public_Service "Pulitzer Prize for Public Service") for his coverage of the [Watergate scandal](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal"), attended the university but did not graduate.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/UMD_Arch_gateway.JPG/250px-UMD_Arch_gateway.JPG)](/wiki/File:UMD_Arch_gateway.JPG)

An arched gateway on campus, located between Montgomery Hall and South Campus Commons #3

Attendees within the fields of science and mathematics are Nobel laureates [Raymond Davis Jr.](/wiki/Raymond_Davis_Jr. "Raymond Davis Jr."), 2002 winner in Physics; [Herbert Hauptman](/wiki/Herbert_Hauptman "Herbert Hauptman"), 1985 winner in Chemistry, and [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal") winner [Charles Fefferman](/wiki/Charles_Fefferman "Charles Fefferman"). Other alumni include [George Dantzig](/wiki/George_Dantzig "George Dantzig"), considered the father of linear programming; late [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") [astronaut](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut") [Judith Resnik](/wiki/Judith_Resnik "Judith Resnik"), who died in the destruction of the [Space Shuttle Challenger](/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Challenger "Space Shuttle Challenger") during the launch of mission [STS-51-L](/wiki/STS-51-L "STS-51-L"); and [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") Administrator [Michael D. Griffin](/wiki/Michael_D._Griffin "Michael D. Griffin").

Several donors have distinguished themselves for their sizable gifts to the university. Businessman [Robert H. Smith](/wiki/Robert_H._Smith_(philanthropist) "Robert H. Smith (philanthropist)"), who graduated from the university in 1950 with a degree in accounting, gave over $45 million to the business school that now bears his name and to the Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, which bears his wife's name.[[162]](#cite_note-rhsmith_2005-164) Construction entrepreneur [A. James Clark](/wiki/A._James_Clark "A. James Clark"), who graduated with an engineering degree in 1950, donated over $45 million to the college of engineering, which also bears his name.[[162]](#cite_note-rhsmith_2005-164) Another engineering donor, [Jeong H. Kim](/wiki/Jeong_H._Kim "Jeong H. Kim"), earned his Ph.D. from the university in 1991 and gave $5 million for the construction of a state-of-the-art engineering building.[[163]](#cite_note-165) [Philip Merrill](/wiki/Philip_Merrill "Philip Merrill"), a media figure, donated $10 million to the College of Journalism.[[164]](#cite_note-166) [Robert E. Fischell](/wiki/Robert_E._Fischell "Robert E. Fischell"), physicist, inventor, and holder of more than 200 U.S. and foreign medical patents[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-MDDI_Fischell-169) donated $30 million to the [A. James Clark School of Engineering](/wiki/A._James_Clark_School_of_Engineering "A. James Clark School of Engineering"),[[168]](#cite_note-170) establishing the Fischell Department of Bioengineering. [Brendan Iribe](/wiki/Brendan_Iribe "Brendan Iribe"), a co-founder of [Oculus VR](/wiki/Oculus_VR "Oculus VR"), donated $31 million to the university in 2014 towards [a new computer science building](/wiki/Iribe_Center "Iribe Center") and scholarships.[[169]](#cite_note-171)
